mnemonics.title: AGILE Values Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember AGILE = A G I L E: Always Get Individuals Living Energy (Individuals & interactions > processes & tools; Working software > comprehensive documentation; Customer collaboration > contract negotiation; Responding to change > following a plan)

mnemonics.title: Scrum Roles Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: POSH Team: Product Owner (P), Scrum Master (S), Dev Team (H) - like a 'posh' team serving the project. Remember: PO decides WHAT, SM enables HOW, Team does THE WORK.

mnemonics.title: Waterfall Phases Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember REQ DEM IMP VER MAIN: Requirements → Design → Implementation → Verification → Maintenance. Like water falling through stages sequentially.

acronyms.usage: Used in Kanban to limit the amount of work in any workflow stage, improving efficiency and reducing bottlenecks
The person responsible for maximizing product value and managing the Product Backlog in Scrum
The servant-leader who facilitates Scrum processes and removes impediments for the team
An Agile software development framework emphasizing technical practices and high-quality code
A shared understanding of what it means for work to be complete, ensuring quality and consistency
The smallest product version that can be released to validate assumptions with real customers

visualTips.visualization: Picture a conveyor belt (Sprint) with boxes (tasks) moving through stations. Each station = a ceremony or deliverable. The belt keeps循环 (loops) every 2-4 weeks.
Imagine a physical post-it board with 3 columns: To Do → In Progress → Done. Arrows show work flowing left to right. WIP limits prevent any column from overflowing.
Traditional = detailed blueprint drawn completely before building. Agile = sketch initial plan, then iterate and refine as construction progresses.
Think of climbing a spiral staircase - each loop (iteration/sprint) builds on the previous one, getting closer to the goal (delivered product).

analogies.analogy: Like following a recipe exactly - you measure everything first, follow steps in order, and the dish turns out as planned (or fails if ingredients change).
Like building with LEGO bricks - you have a general idea of what you're making, but you can adapt and improve as you go, adding pieces based on feedback.
Think of a restaurant menu - items are prioritized (appetizers first, then mains), customers (stakeholders) give feedback, and the menu (backlog) evolves.
Like a 2-week TV series production cycle - each week a new episode (increment) is produced, reviewed by producers (stakeholders), then the next cycle begins.
Like a football team's coach - doesn't play the game (doesn't write code) but removes obstacles, guides the team, and ensures everyone follows game rules (Agile practices).
